claim: The three alloys were vacuum cast, homogenized at 1200 °C for 20 h, hot rolled, and annealed for 1 h at temperatures 1040 °C (Base), 1075 °C (2.5 Si), 1060 °C (2.5 Al), obtaining a grain size ≈140 μm.
evidence_text: The three steels were vacuum cast, homogenized for 20 h at 1200 °C, hot rolled and then annealed for 1 h at the temperatures indicated in Table 1 to achieve coarse grain sizes of approximately 140 lm.

claim: The lattice parameter a0 was obtained from the first four austenite peaks ((111), (200), (220), (311)) by linear regression extrapolation of a0 vs cos²θ/sinθ to θ=90°, with weighted regression favoring high-angle peaks.
evidence_text: The lattice parameters (a0) determined from the first four austenite peaks ((1 1 1), (2 0 0), (2 2 0) and (3 1 1)) were plotted against cos²θ/sinh, and the a0 for each specimen was determined from a linear regression extrapolation to θ = 90° [31]. The a0 vs. cos²θ/sinh regression analysis was weighted to favor higher angle peaks by using quadruplicate data points for (3 1 1) peaks, triplicate data points for (2 2 0) peaks, duplicate data points for (2 0 0) peaks and single data points for (1 1 1) peaks [32].

claim: Al addition (2.5 wt.%) increases the austenite lattice parameter by 1.41×10⁻³ nm (from 0.3585 nm base to 0.3598 nm), whereas 2.5 wt.% Si has a negligible effect (a0=0.3587 nm), as shown in Figure 2 and Table 2.
evidence_text: Figure 2 and Table 2 show the calculated austenite lattice parameter as a function of relative Si and Al alloying. Figure 2 indicates that 2.5 wt.% Al increases the austenite lattice parameter by 1.41 × 10⁻³ nm relative to the base alloy, while 2.5 wt.% Si has a minimal effect.

claim: The linear lattice parameter coefficients A_LP are -0.018×10⁻³ nm/wt% for Si and 0.582×10⁻³ nm/wt% for Al, consistent with literature (Table 2).
evidence_text: The calculated A^LP for Si and Al are 0.018 and 0.582 1 0^{-3} nm wt.%^{-1}, respectively. These values agree well with those reported by Dyson and Holmes [16] (Table 2).

claim: Excluding Al contribution in lattice-parameter-based carbon estimation leads to an overestimate δ_C = x_Al(A_Al^LP/A_C^LP); for x_Al=1 wt.% and literature A_C^LP, δ_C ranges 0.125–0.176 wt.%.
evidence_text: If Al alloying is ignored, the overestimate of austenite C content, δ_C, can be expressed as δ_C = x_Al (A_Al^LP / A_C^LP). Using the value of A_Al^LP found in this study, the range of A_C^LP values reported in the literature, and assuming x_Al is 1 wt.%, the value of δ_C ranges from 0.125 to 0.176 wt.%.

claim: SFE specimens: 12.7 mm cylinders compressed 5% true strain, slices at 45° electropolished (95% acetic, 5% perchloric). TEM at 120 kV with WBDF g3g (220) reflections (s_g≈0.22 nm⁻¹) on {111} grains near foil plane.
evidence_text: The cylinders were compressed to 5% true strain to introduce dislocations into the specimens. Thin slices were then sectioned from the cylinders at 45° from the compression axis and prepared for TEM by grinding and electropolishing with 95% acetic acid and 5% perchloric acid. TEM investigation was performed in a Phillips CM12 TEM operating at 120 kV. For each material, grains with {1 11} planes nearly aligned with the plane of the foil were used for weak-beam dark-field (WBDF) imaging of dissociated dislocations [33,34]. The WBDF imaging was performed using g3g conditions with (220)-type reflections (s_g ≈ 0.22 nm⁻¹).

claim: SFE was calculated from d and α using Eq. (1), with experimental a0, shear modulus G=74 GPa, ν=0.3 (isotropic); elastic anisotropy correction was not applied in the primary analysis.
evidence_text: The isotropic shear modulus (G) and Poisson ratio (m) were assumed to be the same for each material, and were 74 GPa and 0.3, respectively [36]. Since elastic anisotropy was not evaluated in this study ... the isotropic elastic properties were used in the SFE calculations.

claim: The base SFE is 26.3 mJ/m². 2.5% Al addition increases SFE by 12.0 mJ/m², and 2.5% Si decreases it by 6.4 mJ/m², as per Figure 3b.
evidence_text: The base alloy SFE is 26.3 mJ m⁻², which is consistent with the value of 30 mJ m⁻² found for a similar 15 Ni–11 Cr alloy... Figure 3(b) indicates that 2.5 wt.% Al alloying increases the SFE by 12.0 mJ m⁻² from the base alloy value whereas 2.5 wt.% Si alloying decreases it by 6.4 mJ m⁻².

claim: The linear SFE coefficient for Si is -2.6 mJ/m²/wt% (consistent with literature range -1 to -3.5), and for Al is 4.9 mJ/m²/wt% (agreeing with thermochemical 5.2 and XRD 6.4), Table 2.
evidence_text: The values of A_Si^SFE reported in the high-Mn steel literature vary from 1 to 3.5 mJ m⁻² wt.%⁻¹, which is in good agreement with the value -2.6 mJ m⁻² wt.%⁻¹ found in this study (Table 2). Thermochemical modeling of the SFE in high-Mn alloys predicts an A_Al^SFE value of approximately 5.2 mJ m⁻² wt.%⁻¹, which is in very good agreement with the value of 4.9 mJ m⁻² wt.%⁻¹ found in this study (Table 2).

claim: An upper limit estimate of the strain field correction [34] would increase the 2.5 Al alloy SFE to 44.0±9.8 mJ/m², while other alloys change ≤1 mJ/m².
evidence_text: An upper limit estimation suggests that the biggest change is in the 2.5 Al alloy, where the correction would increase the SFE of the 2.5 Al alloy to 44.0 ± 9.8 mJ m⁻². The SFEs of the other alloys would change by approximately 1 mJ m⁻² or less.

claim: Lower SFE promotes planar shear bands and strain-induced martensite; Al increases SFE thereby suppressing shear band formation, while Si decreases SFE enhancing such transformations.
evidence_text: Lower austenite SFE values promote the formation of planar shear bands, including epsilon martensite, the intersections of which may serve as α' martensite nuclei [20]. Increasing the austenite SFE is expected to suppress shear band formation and strain-induced martensite formation.
